title: Make Your Case
developmental_frame: At 9–10, children often ask for things by whining, demanding, or repeating 'pleeease'. They're ready to learn something better: making a calm, clear case with real reasons. They can hold two or three reasons in mind and, with help, anticipate the grown-up's likely 'but'. Real stakes make this land — a later bedtime, a pet, a class idea. Having learned to listen in Q2, they now learn to be worth listening to.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is being taken seriously — getting a fair hearing, and sometimes a yes, because you made a thoughtful case instead of a fuss. It's the discovery that good reasons, said calmly, are far more powerful than volume.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child argues for something they genuinely want, and finds that a calm, reasoned case gets them taken seriously in a way whining never does. The pull is real influence over their own life — being heard and treated like someone with sensible reasons. That taste of being taken seriously makes them want to do it well.

artifact: A real case the child made to a real person (a parent or teacher) for something they want, using two or three reasons and a response to the likely 'but'.
success_criteria: The child gave two or three real reasons, not just 'because I want it'.
They thought about the other person's likely worry and had a response.
They made the case calmly and can say how it was received.

lessons.title: A good reason beats a whine
lessons.concept: Whining and demanding make people say no faster. A calm, clear reason makes them actually think about it. The secret to getting a fair hearing isn't being louder — it's being reasonable.
lessons.real_example: 'Can I stay up later, PLEEEASE?' gets a quick no. 'Could I stay up 15 minutes later on Fridays, since there's no school the next day?' gets a real 'hmm, let me think.' The reason changed everything.
lessons.apply_task: Think of one real thing you'd like to ask for. Write it as a calm request instead of a demand.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How do you usually ask for things — calmly or with a fuss? Which do you think works better?
lessons.feedback_task: Try your calm version on a parent (just the request, no reasons yet) and notice if they listen differently.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a sibling the difference between whining for something and asking calmly.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is about your real wish and your real family — you know the situation. Frame your own request; it means more coming from you.
success_criteria: A real want is framed as a calm request rather than a demand.

lessons.title: Give two or three reasons
lessons.concept: 'Because I want it' isn't a reason — it's a wish. Real reasons explain *why* it's a good idea, ideally good for others too, not just you. Two or three solid reasons make a case hard to wave away.
lessons.real_example: For the later Friday bedtime: 1) no school next day, 2) I'll still get enough sleep, 3) we could use it as family film time. Three reasons — and one even helps the whole family.
lessons.apply_task: Write two or three real reasons for your request. Try to make at least one good for someone besides you.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which of your reasons is strongest? Reasons that help others too are often the most convincing.
lessons.feedback_task: Test your reasons on someone neutral. Ask which reason they find most convincing.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a friend find a 'good for others too' reason for something they want.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Come up with your own reasons first — thinking of *why* is the real skill. If you ask AI, you must believe the reasons yourself, or they'll sound fake when you say them.
success_criteria: Two or three genuine reasons are written, ideally one benefiting others.

lessons.title: Answer the 'but'
lessons.concept: The person you're asking will have a worry — a 'but'. If you think of it first and have an answer ready, your case gets much stronger. It shows you've thought about their side, not just yours.
lessons.real_example: The 'but' for a later bedtime is 'but you'll be tired'. Your answer: 'I'll have a quiet wind-down and still wake up fine — we can try it one Friday and see.' You met their worry head-on.
lessons.apply_task: Guess the other person's biggest 'but'. Write it down, then write your calm answer to it.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was it easy to guess their worry? Thinking about the other person's view is a real thinking skill (and it's kind).
lessons.feedback_task: Check your guessed 'but' with someone who knows the person. Did you guess their worry right?
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how guessing the 'but' makes a request stronger.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Guessing what a *specific real person* worries about needs your knowledge of them — you know your parent or teacher far better than any computer does.
success_criteria: The likely objection is anticipated and answered.

lessons.title: Make your case for real
lessons.concept: Now put it together and actually make your case: calm request, your reasons, your answer to the 'but'. Then — importantly — listen to the reply gracefully, even if it's not a full yes. A good case-maker is always respectful.
lessons.real_example: You make your case well and get 'let's try it for two Fridays and see'. That's a win! Even a 'not yet' often comes with a reason you can work with next time.
lessons.apply_task: Make your real case to the real person, calmly. Afterward, write down how it went and what they said.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did making a calm, reasoned case feel compared to how you'd normally ask? What was the result?
lessons.feedback_task: Whatever the answer, ask the person: 'Did I make my case well?' Their feedback helps you next time.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the story of your case — your reasons, the 'but', and how it went.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is a real, calm conversation between you and a real person — no script can do it for you. Listening well to their reply is as important as your reasons.
success_criteria: The case was made calmly to a real person.
The child listened to the response and can describe how it went.
